questions.question: Under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 (HASAWA), what is the employer's core duty?
options: To avoid providing any lifting equipment in order to reduce costs
To move every person using a hoist, regardless of their ability
To protect the health, safety and welfare of staff and anyone affected by their work, so far as is reasonably practicable
To lift people manually whenever it is quicker than fetching equipment

questions.question: Which body inspects care services to check that staff are trained, risks are assessed, and people are moved safely and with dignity?
options: The National Manual Handling Board
The Royal College of Nursing
The Care Quality Commission (CQC)
The local fire and rescue service

questions.question: In a TILE risk assessment, which of these does the 'Task' element prompt you to consider?
options: The floor surface and the lighting in the room
The person's weight and their ability to help
Your own fitness, training and health
Whether the move involves twisting, stooping, reaching or repetition

questions.question: In a TILE risk assessment, the 'Load' element mainly refers to what?
options: The person or object being moved, including their weight, ability to help and any medical devices such as a catheter
The amount of equipment stored in the room
The employer's overall legal responsibilities
The number of staff working on shift that day

questions.question: What does the 'never lift' (minimal lifting) approach mean in modern care?
options: People should never be moved out of their bed
Staff should manually lift people whenever the person asks them to
Staff should not manually lift the full weight of a person unless there is no safe alternative, such as a genuine emergency
Staff should never use hoists or any other equipment

questions.question: If a person begins to fall while you are assisting them, what is the safest response?
options: Do not fight it; protect their head, guide them gently towards the floor and lower yourself with them
Grab them firmly under the arms and haul them back upright
Twist your body sharply to swing them back into the chair
Step away quickly so that they are not close to you

questions.question: Once a person is on the floor after a fall and it is safe to lift them, how should they be raised?
options: By dragging them across the floor onto a chair
By two staff pulling on their arms
By using a suitable hoist, such as a specialist raising or floor hoist
By a single carer lifting them using good posture

questions.question: Why should you never manually lift a person up from the floor by hand after a fall?
options: Because it simply takes longer than using a hoist
Because the person will always refuse to be helped
Because their care plan forbids them from ever being moved again
Because they may have a fracture or head injury that is not obvious, so a manual lift risks serious harm

questions.question: What is a transfer board used for?
options: Bridging a small gap, for example between a wheelchair and a bed, for someone who can shuffle across
Taking the full weight of a person who cannot bear any weight at all
Repositioning someone up the bed without dragging them
Supporting a person to rise to standing from a chair

questions.question: A stand aid or standing hoist is designed to do what?
options: Support a person who can take some weight to rise and transfer
Bridge the gap between a bed and a wheelchair
Lift the full weight of a person who cannot weight-bear at all
Slide a person up the bed using low friction

questions.question: A person of larger body size with bariatric needs may require what?
options: Standard equipment used beyond its stated safe working load
Specialist bariatric equipment rated for a higher safe working load, more space and often more staff
No equipment at all, as manual lifting is safer for them
Less space and fewer staff than usual

questions.question: What is a dynamic risk assessment in moving and handling?
options: An assessment that only the CQC is allowed to carry out
The six-monthly LOLER examination of a hoist
A written plan that, once agreed, never needs changing
A check you make in the moment, before every move, that the plan still fits what you see today

questions.question: Which organisation regards the drag lift (underarm lift) as a form of abuse?
options: The Care Quality Commission
The Health and Safety Executive
The Royal College of Nursing
The local authority safeguarding team

questions.question: According to the material, how do most back injuries in care actually arise?
options: From a build-up of small, awkward strains over months, such as bending, twisting and reaching
From spending too much time sitting down
From using hoists too frequently
From a single dramatic lift

questions.question: A carer can safely catch a falling adult provided they use good posture.
options: True
False

questions.question: Under the 'never lift' approach, staff use equipment and encourage the person to do what they safely can for themselves.
options: True
False

questions.question: You may use any piece of moving-and-handling equipment as long as it looks in good working order, even if you have never been trained on it.
options: True
False

questions.question: A hoist that has no current LOLER inspection record should never be used.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are now considered dangerous or banned techniques, except in true life-or-death emergencies? (Select all that apply)
options: The drag lift, hooking your arms under a person's armpits to haul them up
The orthodox or cradle lift, where a person clings around the carer's neck
Pulling a person up by their arms out of a chair or bed
Using a hoist and sling to move someone who cannot weight-bear
Using a slide sheet to reposition someone in bed

questions.question: In a TILE assessment, which of these fall under the 'Individual' element — your own capability? (Select all that apply)
options: Your training and experience
Your own fitness and health
The weight of the person being moved
The floor surface and clutter in the room
Whether you are pregnant or have an injury

questions.question: Which of these are correct actions to take once a person is on the floor after a fall? (Select all that apply)
options: Drag them up by their arms onto a chair
Immediately lift them by hand to get them up quickly
Call for medical help or an ambulance if they are hurt, unwell or you are unsure
Record an accurate account and report the fall so the cause can be reviewed
Check them for injury and keep them calm and warm

questions.question: Which of these are recognised principles when planning a common handling task? (Select all that apply)
options: Choose a quicker manual method to save time
Twist at the waist to turn quickly while holding the person
Explain what will happen and agree a clear signal so you move together
Read the person's handling plan before you start
Let the person do what they safely can for themselves

questions.question: The Royal College of Nursing regards the drag lift as a form of ___.
Modern care follows a '___ lift' (minimal lifting) approach, meaning staff avoid manually lifting a person's full weight wherever there is a safe alternative.
This is awareness training only; before you carry out any handling task for real, you must complete practical training and be assessed as ___ by a qualified trainer.
